perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term "Target Company" shall mean ClubHouse Hotels, Inc. prior to the consummation of the Merger and as the surviving corporation in the Merger.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term "Target Company" shall mean ClubHouse Hotels, Inc. regardless of the consummation of the Merger and will continue to be referred to as such indefinitely, even if it is dissolved or renamed.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining 'Target Company' to persist even after dissolution or renaming contradicts standard legal and business practices where a company's identity changes upon such events. This creates uncertainty about which entity the contract actually applies to, particularly concerning obligations and rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law § 278 (Continuation of Corporation After Dissolution for Purposes of Suit and Winding Up Affairs)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts Delaware law (where the Acquiror is incorporated) because it attempts to define the 'Target Company' in a way that ignores the legal consequences of a merger or dissolution. Delaware law § 278 addresses the continuation of a corporation after dissolution for specific purposes like lawsuits and winding up affairs, but it doesn't support the idea of the company continuing indefinitely with the same identity regardless of its legal status.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 251 Â§ 252 Â§ 253 Â§ 254 Â§ 255 Â§ 256 Â§ 257 Â§ 258 Â§ 259 Â§ 260 Â§ 261 Â§ 262 Â§ 263 Â§ 264 Â§ 265 Â§ 266 Â§ 267 Â§ 268 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IX. Merger, Consolidation or Conversion Â§ 251. Merger or consolidation of domestic corporations [For application of this section, see 79 Del. Laws, c. 327, Â§ 8; 80 Del. Laws, c. 265, Â§ 17; and 82 Del. Laws, c. 256, Â§ 24]. (a) Any 2 or more corporations of this State may merge into a single surviving corporation, which may be any 1 of the constituent corporations or may consolidate into a new resulting corporation formed by the consolidation, pursuant to an agreement of merger or consolidation, as the case may be, complying and approved in accordance with this section. (b) The board of directors of each corporation which desires to merge or consolidate shall adopt a resolution approving an agreement of merger or consolidation and declaring its advisability. The agreement shall state: (1) The terms and conditions of the merger or consolidation; (2) The mode of carrying the same into effect; (3) In the case of a merger, such amendments or changes in the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation as are desired to be effected by the merger (which amendments or changes may amend and restate the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation in its entirety), or, if no such amendments or changes are desired, a statement that the certificate of incorporation of the surviving corporation shall be its certificate of incorporation; (4) In the case of a consolidation, that the certificate of incorporation of the resulting corporation shall be as is set forth in an attachment to the agreement; (5) The manner, if any, of converting the shares of each of the constituent corporations into shares or other securities of the corporation surviving or resulting from the merger or consolidation, or of cancelling some or all of such share
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Target Company' to persist even after dissolution or renaming, which contradicts standard legal and business practices. This is a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction, since the changed text uses terms that do not match their statutory meaning. The altered definition creates uncertainty about the entity the contract applies to, particularly concerning obligations and rights. Delaware law § 251 addresses the merger of corporations, indicating that a surviving corporation emerges from the merger of two or more corporations. The original text acknowledges this process, whereas the changed text ignores it by stating the Target Company will be referred to as such indefinitely, even if dissolved or renamed. The changed definition undermines the legal framework governing mergers and corporate identity, which is a core concept of corporate law.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Samples agrees that, except for such disclosure as may be required by applicable law, he shall not, directly or indirectly, for himself or through or on behalf of any other person or entity, at any time after the date hereof, without the prior written consent of the Acquiror, reveal, divulge, disclose or communicate to any person, firm, association, corporation or other entity, or use, in any manner whatsoever any know-how, trade secrets, intellectual property rights, marketing and operating techniques, business contacts, client or customer lists, suppliers, technology, contracts or other confidential or proprietary information of the Target Company, any Related Entity or any of their respective affiliates (except Innco Hospitality, Inc. and any entity controlled by Innco Hospitality, Inc. and Transamerican Properties, Inc. and T.I.P. Realty Partners, and any entity controlled by either of them and the Broadway Plaza Suites in Kansas City, Missouri); provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (i) it is a matter of common knowledge or public record or (ii) the Undersigned can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Samples agrees that, except for such disclosure as deemed appropriate by Samples, he shall not, directly or indirectly, for himself or through or on behalf of any other person or entity, at any time after the date hereof, without the prior written consent of the Acquiror, reveal, divulge, disclose or communicate to any person, firm, association, corporation or other entity, or use, in any manner whatsoever any know-how, trade secrets, intellectual property rights, marketing and operating techniques, business contacts, client or customer lists, suppliers, technology, contracts or other confidential or proprietary information of the Target Company, any Related Entity or any of their respective affiliates (except Innco Hospitality, Inc. and any entity controlled by Innco Hospitality, Inc. and Transamerican Properties, Inc. and T.I.P. Realty Partners, and any entity controlled by either of them and the Broadway Plaza Suites in Kansas City, Missouri); provided, however, that for purposes hereof, information shall not be considered to be confidential or proprietary if (i) it is a matter of common knowledge or public record or (ii) the Undersigned can demonstrate that such information was already known to the recipient thereof other than by reason of any breach of any obligation under this Agreement or any other confidentiality or non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'as may be required by applicable law' to 'as deemed appropriate by Samples' allows Samples to subjectively decide what information he needs to disclose, overriding mandatory disclosure requirements under various laws. This alteration introduces potential conflict of interest and obstructs transparency in information handling.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows Samples to determine what disclosures are 'appropriate', which directly contradicts the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). DTSA protects trade secrets by imposing obligations of confidentiality. If Samples subjectively decides that disclosure of trade secrets is 'appropriate,' even when it violates the DTSA, the contract conflicts with federal law. The DTSA does not allow for subjective interpretation; it sets clear standards for protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text requires disclosure as mandated by 'applicable law,' aligning with legal obligations. However, the modified text substitutes this objective standard with Samples' subjective judgment ('as deemed appropriate by Samples'). This alteration directly contradicts the intent of laws like the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which sets explicit standards for trade secret protection, as stated in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet defines key terms related to trade secret protection, supporting the assertion that the DTSA aims to provide clear, objective standards rather than subjective interpretations of what constitutes appropriate disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Samples agrees that, for a period of five (5) years (or such lesser period hereinafter provided for) following the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), he shall not, without the prior express written consent of the Acquiror: (i) except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below), call upon, solicit, divert, take away or attempt to call upon, solicit, divert or take away any existing or potential customers, suppliers or accounts of the Acquiror Companies, the -2- <PAGE> 3 Target Company or the Related Entities or their respective businesses in connection with any business substantially similar to the Business; (ii) hire or attempt to hire, for himself or on behalf of any other person, any present or future employee of the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company or any Related Entity; or (iii) own, lease, maintain, operate, franchise, license, manage, invest in or provide financing for, or give any advice to any person, firm, partnership, association, venture, corporation or other entity owning an interest in or engaging, directly or indirectly, in the management or operation of, (A) any Hilton Garden, Courtyard by Marriott or Doubletree Club hotel wherever located or (B) any other Upscale Hotel (as defined below) or Extended Stay Hotel (as defined below) located in the United States, Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel now or hereafter operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand, except for any such activity conducted for or on behalf of, or in conjunction with, any of the Acquiror Companies or, in the case of clause (B), Permitted Hotels (as defined below); provided, that if a hotel operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand is announced for a location that is within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel that prior to the date of such announcement was in operation and is owned, managed or operated by any such entity in which Samples has also prior to such date commenced involvement of a type that would otherwise be restricted by clause (B), then this clause (iii) shall not require Samples to cease such involvement; or (iv) enter into any contract or make any commitment to take any action that is restricted by clauses (i), (ii) or (iii) above.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Samples agrees that, for a period of five (5) years (or such lesser period hereinafter provided for) following the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), he shall not, without the prior express written consent of the Acquiror, unless such consent is unreasonably withheld: (i) except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below), call upon, solicit, divert, take away or attempt to call upon, solicit, divert or take away any existing or potential customers, suppliers or accounts of the Acquiror Companies, the -2- <PAGE> 3 Target Company or the Related Entities or their respective businesses in connection with any business substantially similar to the Business; (ii) hire or attempt to hire, for himself or on behalf of any other person, any present or future employee of the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company or any Related Entity; or (iii) own, lease, maintain, operate, franchise, license, manage, invest in or provide financing for, or give any advice to any person, firm, partnership, association, venture, corporation or other entity owning an interest in or engaging, directly or indirectly, in the management or operation of, (A) any Hilton Garden, Courtyard by Marriott or Doubletree Club hotel wherever located or (B) any other Upscale Hotel (as defined below) or Extended Stay Hotel (as defined below) located in the United States, Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel now or hereafter operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand, except for any such activity conducted for or on behalf of, or in conjunction with, any of the Acquiror Companies or, in the case of clause (B), Permitted Hotels (as defined below); provided, that if a hotel operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand is announced for a location that is within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel that prior to the date of such announcement was in operation and is owned, managed or operated by any such entity in which Samples has also prior to such date commenced involvement of a type that would otherwise be restricted by clause (B), then this clause (iii) shall not require Samples to cease such involvement; or (iv) enter into any contract or make any commitment to take any action that is restricted by clauses (i), (ii) or (iii) above.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless such consent is unreasonably withheld' after the 'prior express written consent of the Acquiror' clause can create a legal ambiguity that might render the non-compete agreement unenforceable under Delaware law. The original text unequivocally requires prior written consent, but the added phrase introduces a condition that could be interpreted as a breach of good faith and fair dealing, potentially invalidating the restriction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding restrictive covenants and 'reasonableness'.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware jurisprudence on restrictive covenants, implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware law allows for restrictive covenants, they must be 'reasonable' in scope and duration. By adding 'unless such consent is unreasonably withheld,' the contract introduces an element of subjectivity and potential for dispute about what constitutes 'unreasonable' withholding. This ambiguity conflicts with the general requirement for clarity and certainty in contracts, particularly restrictive covenants. A court might find this clause makes the non-compete agreement too uncertain to be enforced effectively, effectively contradicting established Delaware case law on the need for 'reasonableness' in such agreements.

perturbation.justification: The addition of 'unless such consent is unreasonably withheld' fundamentally alters the enforceability of the non-compete agreement. The original text provides for clear prior express written consent, removing any ambiguity about Samples' obligations. The change introduces subjectivity, contradicting the need for clear and reasonable restrictive covenants, as stated in the Law Explanation, even without direct support from the scraped snippets. This subjectivity creates potential disputes and undermines the certainty required for such agreements to be legally sound, fitting the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED, ENFORCED, AND GOVERNED BY THE INTERNAL LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE (WITHOUT REGARD TO ITS CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES).
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED, ENFORCED, AND GOVERNED BY THE INTERNAL LAWS OF THE STATE OF KANSAS (WITHOUT REGARD TO ITS CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES), EXCEPT FOR MATTERS RELATING TO CORPORATE GOVERNANCE WHICH SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF NEVADA.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause is changed to introduce Nevada law for corporate governance, while the Target Company is a Kansas corporation and the Acquiror is a Delaware corporation. This creates conflict because corporate governance is usually governed by the state of incorporation, thus adding unnecessary ambiguity and potential legal challenges regarding which state's laws control.
perturbation.law_citation: CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America, 481 U.S. 69 (1987)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces Nevada law for corporate governance, which contradicts the established Internal Affairs Doctrine. This doctrine, recognized in *CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America*, holds that the law of the state of incorporation governs internal corporate affairs. By designating Nevada law, the clause creates a direct conflict with this principle, as the Acquiror is a Delaware corporation and the Target Company is a Kansas corporation. The internal affairs of those companies should be governed by Delaware and Kansas law respectively, not Nevada law, thus introducing uncertainty and potential legal challenges.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America | 481 U.S. 69 (1987) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America, 481 U.S. 69 (1987) Overview Opinions Materials Argued: March 2, 1987 Decided: April 21, 1987 Syllabus U.S. Supreme Court CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America, 481 U.S. 69 (1987) CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corporation of America No. 86-71 Argued March 2, 1987 Decided April 21, 1987* 481 U.S. 69 Syllabus The federal Williams Act and implementing regulations govern hostile corporate stock tender offers by requiring, inter alia, that offers remain open for at least 20 business days. An Indiana Act applies to certain business corporations chartered in Indiana that have specified levels of shares or shareholders within the State and that opt into the Act's protection. The Indiana Act provides that the acquisition of "control shares" in such a corporation -- shares that, but for the Act, would bring the acquiring entity's voting power to or above certain threshold levels -- does not include voting rights unless a majority of all preexisting disinterested shareholders so agree at their next regularly scheduled meeting. However, the stock acquiror can require a special meeting within 50 days by following specified procedures. Appellee Dynamics Corporation announced a tender offer that would have raised its ownership interest in CTS Corporation above the Indiana Act's threshold. Dynamics also filed suit in Federal District Court alleging federal securities violations by CTS. After CTS opted into the Indiana Act, Dynamics amended its complaint to challenge the Act's validity. The District Court granted Dynamics' motion for declaratory relief, ruling that the Act is preempted by the Williams Act, and violates the Commerce Clause. The Court of Appeals affirmed, adopting the holding of the plurality opinion in Edgar v. MITE Corp., 457 U. S. 624 , that the Williams Act preempts state statutes that upset the balance between target company management and a tender offeror. The court based its preemption finding
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces Nevada law for corporate governance, directly conflicting with the Internal Affairs Doctrine. As the Law Explanation clarifies, this doctrine, supported by *CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America*, mandates that the law of the state of incorporation governs internal corporate affairs. The changed text specifies Nevada law for corporate governance while the Target Company is a Kansas corporation and the Acquiror is a Delaware corporation, violating this doctrine. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing *CTS Corp. v. Dynamics Corp. of America*, reinforces the significance of this case law. This misalignment introduces ambiguity and potential legal challenges, as corporate governance is typically governed by the state of incorporation.
